text: The robotic thymectomy begins with the patient positioned supine, a pillow placed between the shoulder blades, and the chest accessed from the left side using three ports. Dissection starts lateral to the left internal mammary artery (LIMA), moving superiorly towards the cervical portion of the thymus. The LIMA and its vein are visible, along with the phrenic nerve and innominate vein. The surgeon carefully dissects, stripping thymic fat from the innominate vein and peeling perithymic fat from the pericardium. Scissors are used to dissect medial to the left phrenic nerve, avoiding thermal injury. The procedure involves removing the thymus en bloc, along with perithymic fat and tissues from the left to the right phrenic nerve, extending from the superior horn near the innominate vein and superior vena cava (SVC) down to the diaphragm.

The surgeon uses traction and contraction techniques combined with diathermy and scissors to peel off fat from the innominate vein, revealing the accessory horn of the thymus located just inferior to the vein. The lung collapse is maintained using a double lumen tube and carbon dioxide insufflation.

The surgeon uses robotic instruments to clip or coagulate the thymic bivane with diathermy or a combination of diathermy and scissors, demonstrating flexibility and a wide range of movements. The chest cavity is inspected for any remaining thymic tissue or ectopic thymus and fat.
The surgeon removes the thymus from the chest cavity using an endo seal bag through one of the surgical ports. Hemostasis is achieved, and a chest drain is inserted before closing the surgical site.
